# AirPodsReconnectFix (iOS 6)

Fixes **AirPods Pro 2/3 on iOS 6** that pair fine but then disconnect and
reconnect every few seconds after the post-AirPods-Pro-3 firmware update — and
keeps audio routing to them reliably once they stay connected.

To iOS 6 the AirPods are just a generic A2DP/HFP Bluetooth headset (it has none
of the W1/AAP machinery modern iOS uses). The newer firmware expects the host to
periodically re-affirm the connection — something current iOS does automatically
but the ancient iOS 6 stack never does — so the AirPods tear the link down on a
fixed timer, then immediately re-pair, over and over.

## What it does

Two complementary keep-alives, both running in SpringBoard via the private
`BluetoothManager` framework (no daemon injection, no patched system files):

| Problem | Fix |
|---|---|
| **The link drops on a dead-regular ~45 s timer.** Logs show every disconnect landing 42–49 s after the prior connect — a timer firing, not interference. Plain link *traffic* (silent audio, battery polling) does **not** reset it. | **Pre-emptive service re-assert.** Every 18 s while the AirPods are connected, it calls the system's own `-connectWithServices:` on the live device — re-running the exact service-connect handshake the firmware is waiting for, well before the ~45 s deadline. The teardown timer keeps getting reset and never fires. |
| **Audio routing goes stale on gaps.** Once the link stays up, the A2DP stream goes idle between tracks (on a skip) or when music stops, and iOS 6 fails to re-point output back to the AirPods — system sounds / the next song play to nowhere until you manually flip the route (speaker → AirPods). | **Silent A2DP keep-warm stream.** A continuous, inaudible silent PCM loop (`AVAudioSession` Playback + `MixWithOthers`, so it layers under real music and never interrupts it) keeps the stream from ever going idle, so the route never goes stale. |

A **burst-reconnect** safety net remains: if a drop ever slips through, it
re-establishes in ~2 s instead of looping. There's also a manual route-bounce
you can fire by hand (see *Manual trigger* below) replicating the speaker↔AirPods
flip.

## How it was diagnosed

Empirically, from the connect/disconnect logs — not by reading the firmware. The
giveaways:

- The disconnect was a perfect metronome (~45 s, for hours) → a timer, not RF.
- Two "keep the link busy" approaches (periodic state polling, and a continuous
  silent stream à la [asentientbot/ios-6-pods-hack](https://github.com/asentientbot/ios-6-pods-hack))
  both **failed** to stop the drop → the timeout is about re-affirming the
  *service connection*, not about traffic flowing.
- Periodically re-asserting the service connection **stopped the drops** entirely.

The exact Bluetooth-spec mechanism (supervision timeout vs. sniff mode vs.
profile keepalive) is inferred, since it can't be observed from userspace on iOS
6. What's proven is the behaviour: fixed ~45 s drop, traffic-based keepalives
don't help, periodic service re-assert does.

## Install

Add the repo in Cydia/Installer and install **AirPodsReconnectFix**, then respring:

```
https://mikey820.github.io/repo/
```

Requires iOS 6, a jailbreak with `mobilesubstrate`, and AirPods

## Logs (no Mac needed)

`os_log` doesn't exist on iOS 6, so everything is written to a plain text file
you can open in **Filza**:

```
/var/mobile/AirPodsReconnectFix.log
```

Useful lines:

- `[SpringBoard][preempt] RE-ASSERT ...` — the 18 s service re-assert firing.
- `[warm] START / play ...` — the silent keep-warm stream.
- `[SpringBoard][event] disconnect ...` — any actual drop (should be gone).
- `[SpringBoard][mitigation] BURST reconnect ...` — fallback recovery.

## Manual route bounce

If you ever want to force the speaker↔AirPods flip by hand (the original manual
workaround), fire the Darwin notification:

```
notifyutil -p com.mikey820.airpodsreconnectfix.bounce
```

## Tuning (`Tweak.x`)

- `kPreemptIntervalSec` (default `18.0`) — how often to re-assert the service.
  Must stay comfortably below the ~45 s firmware deadline.
- `kAutoReconnectEnabled` — `NO` for a logging-only build (no state changes).
- `kBurstDelaysSec`, `kMaxAttempts`, `kWindowSec` — burst-reconnect fallback.

## Build

iOS 6 is 32-bit (`armv7`), rootful, deployment target 6.0. CI
(`.github/workflows/build.yml`) runs on `ubuntu-latest` (Linux `ld64-port` still
links armv7), pulls an armv7-capable iPhoneOS SDK (`theos/sdks`, 9.3) into Theos
with an empty `liblaunch.tbd` stub, builds `TARGET = iphone:clang:latest:6.0`,
and attaches the `iphoneos-arm` `.deb` to the GitHub Release on any `v*` tag.
Frameworks: `MediaPlayer` (route bounce) + `AVFoundation` (silent keep-warm).

Locally: `make clean all` with `$THEOS` set and an old SDK in `$THEOS/sdks`.
